content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_p99_l2"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">et jam</foreign>), still, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eludet_p99_l2"> , <gloss> baffle</gloss>, i.e. his mad conduct <gloss>makes fools of</gloss> the Roman people, as it were, by continuing to escape the just punishment that would suppress it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_finem_p99_l2">: almost equivalent to <foreign xml:lang="lat">quamdiu</foreign>, but implying some shock or crisis (<foreign xml:lang="lat">finem</foreign>) which must follow. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sese_jactabit_p99_l3"> , <gloss>insolently display itself</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_p99_l3"> (adv. acc.), <gloss>not at all</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Palati_p99_l4">: one of the strongest positions in the city, commanding the Forum, and so most likely to be seized by the conspirators. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">Palatium</foreign>, an isolated hill, of a rudely quadrangular shape, was the original seat of the city of Rome, from which the city spread gradually over the other hills. In the last years of the Republic, the Palatine became the fashionable place for residences. Here was Cicero's house as well as Catiline's. It was because of its nearness to his house, as well as because of the strength of its position, that Cicero selected this temple for the meeting of the Senate on this occasion. Under the Empire the Palatine became the seat of the imperial residence, and its name, <gloss> palace</gloss>, has passed in this sense into most modern languages. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bonorum_p99_l5">: the Senate was surrounded by a crowd of <foreign xml:lang="lat"> equites</foreign> and other citizens (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.21">21</ref>, below). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="locus_p100_l1">: the Senate was assembled, not, as usual, in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Curia Hostilia</foreign>, but in the Temple of Jupiter Stator, which occupied a commanding position on the brow of the Palatine Hill and faced the Sacred Way. The ruins of this temple were discovered some years ago (see view in text). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="horum_p100_l1"> (with a gesture), i.e. the Senators present. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ora_p100_l1"> , <gloss> features</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voltus_p100_l1"> , <gloss> expression</gloss>: the phrase is a sort of hendiadys, almost equivalent to <gloss>expression of their features</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 640">640</ref> (385); B. 374, 4; G. 698; H. 751,3, N.1 (636, iii, 2); H-B. 631,5. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patere_p100_l2">: note the emphatic position. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_p100_l2">: observe the abruptness and force given by omitting the interrog. particle -<foreign xml:lang="lat">ne</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="constrictam_teneri_p100_l2"> , <gloss>is held fast bound</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 497">497</ref>, b (292, c) ; cf. B. 337, 6; G. 238; H.431,1(385, 1, N.); H.-B. 605,5. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proxima_p100_l4"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">superiore</foreign>: for what was done on the night of Nov. 6, see sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.4">4</ref>; as to <foreign xml:lang="lat">proxima</foreign>, <gloss>last night</gloss>, we meet with nothing but general assertions. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempora_p100_l7">, etc., <gloss>what a time! what a state of things!</gloss> (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mores_p100_l7"> <gloss>customs of the time</gloss>.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="immo_p100_l8"> , <gloss>nay more</gloss>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="immo_p100_l8_2"> here negatives not the <gloss>fact</gloss> of the preceding statement (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vivit_p100_l8">), but only its <gloss>form</gloss> as not being strong enough; <gloss> nay</gloss> is similarly used in English, as in <gloss>Midsummer-Night's Dream</gloss>, iii, 2, 313: "To strike me, spurn me, nay, to kill me too." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videmur_p100_l11">, etc. <gloss>think we do enough for</gloss> (i.e. fulfil our duty to the state). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_vitemus_p100_l11">: in the dir. form, <foreign xml:lang="lat">satisfacimus si vitamus</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_mortem_p100_l12">: the consuls originally possessed full powers of judgment in criminal cases, including punishment by death. These highest powers of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign> were suspended within the city by laws which gave the right of appeal to the people (note, <ref n="pl110.16">p. 110, l. 16</ref>), but the Senate could revive them in cases of danger by the formula <foreign xml:lang="lat">Videant consules ne quid res publica detrimenti capiat</foreign>, — a proceeding analogous to the proclamation of martial law. This action the Senate had taken Oct.21, nearly three weeks before. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p100_l13">, apod. of an implied cond.: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304, 3, a; G. 254, R1; H. 583 (511, I, N.1); H-B. 582, 3, a; the imperf. is used with <foreign xml:lang="lat">jam pridem</foreign>, where in English we might expect the pluperf.; § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, b (277, b); B. 260,4; G. 234; H. 535(469,2); H.-B. 485; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p100_l13_2"> alone would mean "you ought [now] to be [but are not]"; with <foreign xml:lang="lat">jam pridem</foreign> it means "you ought to have been long ago and still ought to be." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_diu_p100_l14">: words in brackets are thought to be spurious insertions in the text. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="an_vero_p100_l14"> properly belongs both to <foreign xml:lang="lat">interfecit</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">perferemus</foreign>; in English we should connect the two clauses by <gloss>and</gloss>. On the force of <foreign xml:lang="lat">an</foreign>, see § <ref n="AG 335">335</ref>, b (211, b); B. 162, 4, a; G. 457, 1 ; H. 380, 3 (353, N.4); H.-B. 236. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vir_amplissimus_p100_l14"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">pontifex maximus</foreign>: observe how these words strengthen the force of the example. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ti_Gracchium_p100_l15">: Tiberius Sempronius Gracchus, a young man of high rank and great purity of character, attempted to carry through some important reforms, particularly touching the tenure of the public lands, <date when="-0133">B.C. 133</date>. Requiring more time to make his legislation effective, he attempted illegally to secure his own re-election as tribune, when he was attacked and killed by a mob of Senators headed by P. Scipio Nasica. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="privatus_p100_l16">: at the time referred to, Nasica was only a private citizen of consular rank. He afterwards went into exile, and was made Pontifex Maximus in his absence. The word <foreign xml:lang="lat">privatus</foreign> is rhetorically opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nos_consules_p100_l16"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_p100_l18"> , <gloss>that case</gloss>, plural for singular as referring to the circumstances of the case. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ahala_p100_l19">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">magister equitum</foreign> of the famous Cincinnatus; he killed without legal process the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> eques</foreign> Maelius, on suspicion that the latter was aiming at royal power (<date when="-0439">B.C. 439</date>); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novis_rebus_p100_l19"> (the classic expression for a violent change of government), <gloss> revolution</gloss>: dat. after <foreign xml:lang="lat">studentem</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuit_p100_l20"> (emphat.), <gloss>there was</gloss>, etc., implying that it is so no longer; § <ref n="AG 598">598</ref>, d (344 d, 3). Cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">fuit Ilium</foreign>, <ref n="Verg. A. 2.325">Aeneid 2.325</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="habemus_p100_l22"> (emphat.), i.e. <gloss>it is not that we lack</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatus_consultum_p100_l22">: i.e. the decree conferring dictatorial power on the consuls (see note on sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.2">2</ref>, 1.12, above), <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut videant consules</foreign>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vehemens_p100_l23"> , <gloss>severe</gloss>, as regards Catiline; grave, <gloss>carrying weight</gloss>, and so justifying the consuls in any extreme measures. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_deest_p100_l23">, etc., <gloss>it is not that the state lacks wise counsels</gloss>, etc., but that the consuls are remiss in executing them. <milestone n="2" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decrevit_p100_l26">: translate, to preserve the emphasis, <gloss>there was once a decree</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_videret_p100_l26">, subst. clause of purp., obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">decrevit</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 563">563</ref> (331); B. 295, 4; G. 546; H. 564, i (498); H-B. 502, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Opimius_p100_l26">: Lucius Opimius was consul <date when="-0121">B.C. 121</date>, when Caius Gracchus, the younger brother of Tiberius, was attempting to carry through a series of measures far more revolutionary than those of his brother. The Senate took alarm, and entrusted the consul with absolute power. In the tumult that ensued, some 3,000 are said to have lost their lives, including Gracchus and his leading associate, Fulvius. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_caperet_p100_l27">, obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">videret</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interfectus_est_p100_l28"> (emphat.), i.e. in that case death was promptly inflicted. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patre_p100_l29">: Tiberius Gracchus, the elder, one of the most eminent statesmen of his day. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="avo_p100_l29">: Scipio Africanus, the conqueror of Hannibal. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Mario_p101_l1"> (dat. after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="permissa_p101_l1">): this was in Marius' sixth consulship (<date when="-0100">B.C. 100</date>). He was secretly in league with the revolutionists, —Saturninus and Servilius Glaucia, corrupt demagogues, unworthy imitators of the noble Gracchi. When it came to the point, however, the courage of Marius failed him: he deserted his accomplices, and joined the Senate in crushing the revolt. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rei_publicae_p101_l3">: poss. gen., the punishment being looked on as something belonging to the party avenged, and exacted from the other party as a payment due. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="remorata_est_p101_l4"> (governing Saturninum, etc.), <gloss>keep Saturninus and Servilius waiting</gloss>, i.e. did they have to wait one day, etc.? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vicesimum_p101_l4">: strictly speaking, it was now (Nov. 6) the 19th day by Roman reckoning from Oct. 21; cf. § <ref n="AG 424">424</ref>, c (259, c); G. 336, R.1 </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patimur_p101_l4">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref> (276, a); B. 259, 4; G. 230; H. 532, 2 (467, 2); H.-B. 485. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="horum_p101_l5">, i.e. the Senate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujusce_modi_p101_l6">, i.e. like those just mentioned; § <ref n="AG 146">146</ref>, a, N.1 (101, footnote); B. 87, footnote 2; G. 104, I, N.1; H. 178, 3 (186, I); H.-B. 138, 2, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tabulis_p101_l6">: brazen tablets, on which the laws, etc., were inscribed. The edict is said to be <gloss>shut up</gloss> in them (until put in force), <gloss>like a sword hidden in its scabbard</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interfectum_esse_p101_l8">: § <ref n="AG 486">486</ref>, b and N. (288, d); B. 270, 2, a; G. 280, 2; H-B. 582, 31 a, footnote 2 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="convenit_p101_l8">, perf.: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304,3, a; G. 254, a.1; H. 583(511, I, N.1); H-B. 582,3, a. <pb n="365"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_deponendam_p101_l9">, etc.: § <ref n="AG 506">506</ref> (300); B. 338, 3; G. 432; H. 628, 623 (542, iii, 544, I); H.-B. 384, 3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cupio_p101_l10"> (emphat.), <gloss>I am anxious</gloss>: a concession, opposed by <foreign xml:lang="lat">sed</foreign>, below. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_esse_p101_l10">: § <ref n="AG 563">563</ref>, b, I (331, b, N.); B. 331, iv, a; G. 532, R.2; H. 614 (535, ii); H.-B. 586, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dissolutum_p101_l11"> , <gloss>arbitrary</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipse_p101_l12">: Latin in such cases emphasizes the subject; English, the object; § <ref n="AG 298">298</ref>,f(195, 1); B. 249, 2; G. 311, 2; H. 509, I (452, I); H-B. 268. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inertiae_p101_l12">: § <ref n="AG 352">352</ref> (220); B. 228, 2; G. 378; H. 456 (409, U); H.-B. 342. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="castra_sunt_p101_l13">, etc.: an enumeration of the circumstances which make a mild policy no longer possible. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faucibus_p101_l14"> , <gloss>narrow pass</gloss>, leading north from Etruria, through the Apennines. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlocata_p101_l14">: § <ref n="AG 495">495</ref> (291, b); B. 337, 2; G. 250, a.2; H. 538, 4 (47 I, 6, N.1); H.-B. 320, iii, 248. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_p101_l18"> , <gloss>at once</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erit_verendum_p101_l19">, etc., <gloss>I shall have to fear, I suppose</gloss> (ironical), <gloss>that all good citizens will fail to say</gloss> (lit. <gloss>will not say</gloss> ) <gloss>that I have acted too late rather than that anybody will say that I have acted too cruelly</gloss>, i.e. I shall have to fear that I shall be accused of cruelty rather than slackness. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_dicat_p101_l19">: § <ref n="AG 564">564</ref> (331,f); B. 296, 2, a; G. 550, 2; H. 567, 2 (498, iii, N.2); H.-B. 502, 4. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="boni_p101_l19"> (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">dicant</foreign>): here, as usual, <gloss>the well-intentioned</gloss>, i.e. those who held the speaker's views. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_p101_l21">: opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnes_boni_p101_l21"> (1.19, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="factum_esse_p101_l21">: § <ref n="AG 486">486</ref>, b and N. (288, d); B. 270, 2, a; G. 280, a.2; H.-B. 582, 3, a, footnote 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportuit_p101_l21">: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c) ; B. 304, 3, a; G. 597, a.3, a; H. 583 (51 I, I, N.') ; H-B. 582, 3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="denique_p101_l22">, i.e. then, and not before. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_p101_l23"> , <gloss>at length</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fateatur_p101_l24">: for mood, see § <ref n="AG 537">537</ref>, 2 (319, 2); B. 284, 2; G. 631, I; H. 589, ii, 591 (509, 1); H.-B. 521, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_vivis_p101_l26">, just <gloss>as you are</gloss> [now] <gloss>living</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_possis_p101_l27">: purpose (not result). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_p101_l28"> , <gloss> besides</gloss> the forces on guard. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="speculabuntur_p101_l29">: probably referring to the spies in the interest of the government, who were in the very heart of the conspiracy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_amplius_p102_l1">, etc., <gloss>what is there for you to wait for more?</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_exspectes_p102_l1">: rel. clause of purpose. <pb n="366"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inlustrantur_p102_l4">, opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obscurare_p102_l4"> ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erumpunt_p102_l4">, to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="continere_p102_l4"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recognoscas_p102_l7"> , <gloss>review</gloss>, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="licet_p102_l7"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut</foreign> omitted: see § <ref n="AG 565">565</ref> (331, f R.) ; B. 295, 8; G. 553, R.1; H. 564, ii, I (502, I); Cf. H.-B. 502, 3, footnote 1 </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicere_p102_l8">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 584">584</ref>, a and N. (336 A, N.1); G. 281, 2, N. H. 618, 2 (537, I); H.-B. 593, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="futurus_esset_p102_l9">: subord. clause in md. disc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="num_p102_l11">, etc., <gloss>was I mistaken in</gloss>, etc. (lit. <gloss>did the fact escape me</gloss>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_p102_l13"> (nom.) has the force of <gloss>also</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optimatium_p102_l14">, i.e. of the Senatorial party. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_diem_p102_l14">: § <ref n="AG 424">424</ref>, g (259, e); B. 371, 6; G. p.491; H .754, 3 (642, 4); H-B. 668. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sui_causa_p102_l16">: § <ref n="AG 504">504</ref>, b, c (298, a, c); B. 339, 5, 338, I, c; G. 428, a.1 and R.2; H. 626, 3 (542, N.1); H.-B. 614. This passage is neatly turned so as to save their self-respect by attributing their flight to that discretion which is the better part of valor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_dicebas_p102_l19">: we should expect <foreign xml:lang="lat">diceres</foreign>; the imperf. indic. is probably an archaic survival; cf. § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, e and N. (277, e and. N.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p102_l20">: opposed to <foreign xml:lang="lat">discessu</foreign> ("though the rest were gone, yet," etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Praeneste_p102_l21"> (<gloss>Palestrina</gloss>), an important town of Latium, about twenty miles from Rome, in a very commanding situation. Its possession would have given Catiline a valuable military post. Praeneste had been a chief stronghold of the Marian party in the Civil War, and Sulla had punished it by establishing a military colony there (hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">coloniam</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sensistine_p102_l23"> , <gloss>did you not find?</gloss> -<foreign xml:lang="lat"> ne</foreign> here <foreign xml:lang="lat">nonne</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 332">332</ref>, c and N. (210, d and N.); G. 454, N.5; H.-B. 231,1, N.1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praesidiis_p102_l24"> , <gloss>the garrison</gloss> manning the walls; custodus, <gloss> sentinels</gloss> at the gates; vigilus, <gloss>watchmen</gloss> (i.e. night-guard). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agis_p102_l24">, etc.: notice the climax. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="noctem_superiorem_p102_l24"> , <gloss>that night, night before last</gloss>, i.e. Nov. 6; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="priore_p102_l24"> (1.29, below) refers to the same night. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_te_p102_l29">: § <ref n="AG 581">581</ref>, N.2 (336, a, I, R.); H. 643, I (524, 1.1); H.-B. 535, I, c. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inter_falcarios_p102_l30">, i.e. to the street of the scythe-makers. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_obscure_p102_l30">, i.e. I will speak out and be more definite. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_domum_p102_l30">: § <ref n="AG 428">428</ref>, k (258, b, N.3); G. 337, a.3; H-B. 454, 3. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eodem_p103_l1"> , <gloss>at the same place</gloss> (lit. <gloss>to the same place</gloss>, according to the Latin idiom). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gentium_p103_l4">: § <ref n="AG 346">346</ref>, a, 4(216, a, 4); B. 201, 2; G. 372, N.3; H. 443 (397, 4); H.-B. 346. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_republicam_p103_l5"> , <gloss>what sort of state?</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_p103_l6"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">hic</foreign>, <gloss>here, right here</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patres_p103_l6"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">[et]</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conscripti_p103_l6">: the formal designation of the Senators; <foreign xml:lang="lat">patres</foreign> were the patrician members of the Senate, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> conscripti</foreign> were the plebeians <gloss>enrolled</gloss> in that originally patrician body. The conjunction is regularly omitted (as often in such combinations). Observe that the stock English translation <gloss>conscript fathers</gloss> is inexact. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_p103_l8">: the antecedent is the understood subject of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sunt_p103_l8"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_adeo_p103_l8"> , <gloss>and in fact</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cogitent_p103_l9">: § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, a (320, a); B. 283, 2; G. 631, 2; H. 591, 1 (503, 1); H-B. 521, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p103_l11">: see sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.2">2</ref> and note. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voce_volnero_p103_l11">: the alliteration is intentional and may easily be imitated in English, — <gloss>wound with a word</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="igitur_p103_l11"> (resumptive), <gloss>then</gloss> (i.e. <gloss>as I said</gloss> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quemque_p103_l13"> , <gloss> each</gloss> (of the conspirators). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="placeret_p103_l13">, indir. quest. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="relinqueres_p103_l14"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">educeres</foreign>, delib. subj. in an indir. quest.: § <ref n="AG 575">575</ref>, b (334, b); B. 302; G. 265; H. 559, 4 (484, v); H.-B. 503. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="morae_p103_l16">: partitive gen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="viverem_p103_l16">: subj. in subord. clause in indir. disc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equites_p103_l17">: these were C. Cornelius and L. Vargunteius. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnia_comperi_p103_l19">: Cicero's contemporaries made sport of him for using this phrase so often in the case of the conspirators. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salutatum_p103_l22">: supine; § <ref n="AG 509">509</ref> (302); B. 340, I ; G. 435; H. 633 (546); H.-B. 618. All prominent citizens were accustomed to hold a kind of morning reception (cf. "the king's levee") to which their friends and dependents came to bid them good morning and to escort them to the Forum. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_venissent_p103_l22">: best translated by <gloss>when</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_temporis_p103_l23"> , <gloss>at that very time</gloss>: §§ <ref n="AG 346">346</ref>, a, 3, 397, a (216, a, 3, 240, b); B. 201, 2, 185, 2; G. 336, N.2, 369; H. 416, 2, 442 (378, 2, 397, 3); H.-B. 346, 388, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praedixeram_p103_l24">: Cicero had thus put on record, as it were, the fact that he was acquainted with the details of the Conspiracy. <milestone n="103.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="desiderant_p103_l24"> , <gloss>hove been wanting</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref> (276, a); B. 259, 4; G. 230; H. 532, 2 (467, 2); H-B. 485. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_minus_p103_l28"> (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">omnes</foreign>), <gloss>if not</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="murus_p103_l30">, i.e. city wall (cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">parietibus</foreign> , <gloss>walls of a house</gloss>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.6">6</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="intersit_p103_l30">: § <ref n="AG 528">528</ref>(314); B. 310, ii; G. 573; H. 587 (513, i); H-B. 529. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_feram_p103_l31">, etc.: the same idea is repeated for emphasis, but, for variety, different words are used. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_p103_l32"> , <gloss>and particularly</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_p103_l32">, i.e. in whose temple we are met. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Statori_p104_l1"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sto</foreign>): the one who causes to <gloss>stand firm</gloss>. The temple to Jupiter Stator was vowed by Romulus when his troops were giving way, and built upon the spot where their flight was <gloss> stayed</gloss>. The remains of this temple have been recently discovered on the Palatine, near the Arch of Titus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_homine_p104_l3"> , <gloss>by one man</gloss> (Catiline); lit. <gloss>in the case of one man</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proximis_p104_l7">: the consular election was usually held in July; but in this year, on account of the disturbed condition of things, it did not take place until Oct.28. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Campo_p104_l7">: the <gloss> <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia centuriata</foreign> </gloss>, in which the higher magistrates were elected, were held in the Campus Martius, or military parade-ground, north of the city. This is the space covered by the main part of modern Rome. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="competitores_p104_l8">: Catiline's successful competitors were D. Silanus and L. Murena. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="copiis_p104_l9">, i.e. persons in the employ of his friends, — slaves and hired retainers. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullo_concitato_p104_l9"> , <gloss>without exciting</gloss> (a very common way of expressing this idiom in Latin). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videbam_p104_l11"> , <gloss>I saw all along</gloss> (observe the force of the imperf.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_jam_p104_l12"> , <gloss>now at length</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_imperi_p104_l16">, i.e. that which I now possess: namely, that conferred upon the consuls by the special decree of the Senate <foreign xml:lang="lat">dent operant</foreign>, etc. (see note on <ref n="pl100.12">p. 100, l. 12</ref>). Without this decree they possessed <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>, it is true, but it was limited (in the city) by special privileges of Roman citizens. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tu_p104_l20">: opposed to <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitum</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sentina_publicae_p104_l22"> , <gloss>political rabble</gloss>; or, keeping the original figure, we might say, <gloss>bilge-water of the ship of state</gloss> . <milestone n="6" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faciebas_p104_l24"> , <gloss>were on the point of doing</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 471"> 471</ref>, c (277, c); B. 260, 3; G. 233; H. 534, 2 (469, I); H-B. 484. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostem_p104_l25"> , <gloss>a public enemy</gloss>, whom the consul would have the right to expel from the city. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_jubeo_p104_l25">: Cicero avoids the appearance of ordering a citizen to go into exile, since that was something which the consul had no right to do. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_p104_l27"> , <gloss> longer</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="metuat_p104_l29">: cf. note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">cogitent</foreign>, <ref n="pl103.9">p. 103, l. 9</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="privatarum_rerum_p104_l30"> , <gloss>in private life</gloss>, i.e. intercourse with others out of the family (distinguished from <foreign xml:lang="lat"> domesticae</foreign>, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_inretisses_p105_l1">, i.e. <gloss>after entangling</gloss>, etc. (subj. of characteristic). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ferrum_facem_p105_l1">, i.e. arm him for acts of violence, or inflame him to deeds of lust. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_vero_p105_l3"> , <gloss>and sly!</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novis_nuptiis_p105_l4">, etc.: this crime is mentioned by no other writer, and is perhaps one of the orator's exaggerations. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aho_scelere_p105_l5">: Sallust mentions, as a matter of common belief, that Catiline killed his own son, in order to gratify his new wife Aurelia Orestilla, "a woman praised for nothing but beauty." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ruinas_p105_l8">: this charge was undoubtedly correct. The conspiracy was mainly composed of men of ruined fortunes, who hoped to better themselves in the general scramble of a revolution. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Idibus_p105_l9">: the Calends and Ides — the beginning and middle of the month—were the usual times for the payment of debts. Catiline's failure in his consular canvass had probably stirred up his creditors to push him for payment. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_p105_l14">: causal, but best translated by <gloss>when</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pridie_Januarias_p105_l15">, etc.: Dec.31, <date when="-0066">B.C. 66</date>. The act here mentioned seems to have been in preparation for a rising that had been planned by Catiline for the next day, Jan. 1, <date when="-0065">B.C. 65</date>. On this day the consuls Cotta and Torquatus entered upon their office, and it was the intention of Catiline to take advantage of their inauguration to murder them and seize the government. The plot got whispered about, and its execution was put off to Feb. 5, when it failed again through Catiline's precipitancy in giving the word. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_telo_p105_l16"> (a technical expression), <gloss>weapon in hand</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="manum_p105_l17">: a band (of assassins). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interficiendorum_causi_p105_l17">: § <ref n="AG 504">504</ref>, b (298, c); cf. B. 338, I, c; G. 428, R.2; H-B. 612, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mentem_aliquam_p105_l18"> , <gloss>some change of mini</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aut_aut_p105_l20">, etc., <gloss>either obscure or few</gloss>. <pb n="370"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_multa_p105_l21">, etc., i.e. they were too well known to need recapitulation, and too numerous to admit of it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commissa_p105_l21"> , <gloss>which you have perpetrated</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petitiones_p105_l23"> , <gloss>thrusts</gloss>: the word regularly used for the attack of a gladiator. Cicero uses this and similar terms as an affront to Catiline. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_conjectas_p105_l23">, etc., <gloss>so aimed that they seemed impossible to be shunned</gloss>. The Latin has no adj. for "impossible." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="corpore_p105_l24">, i.e. dodging with the body (a common colloquialism —hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut aiunt</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tibi_p105_l26"> (dative of reference), etc., <gloss>wrested from your hands</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 377">377</ref> (235, a); B. 188, I); G. 350, 1; H. 425, 4, N. (384, 4, N.2); H.-B. 368. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_quidem_p105_l28">, etc., <gloss>I know not by what rights it has been consecrated and set apart, that you think</gloss>, etc. (as if Catiline had solemnly pledged himself to use this dagger on nobody lower than a consul). <milestone n="105.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_vero_p105_l28"> , <gloss>but now</gloss> (indicating a marked transition). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vita_p105_l28">, i.e. that you should desire to prolong it (cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.15">15</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_nulla_p106_l1"> , <gloss>nothing of which</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 346">346</ref>, e (216, e); B. 201, I, b; G. 370, R.2; H.-B. 346, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessariis_p106_l3">: this word is used of any close relation, as that of kinsman, client, guest, comrade, member of the same order, etc. (see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">necessitudinem</foreign>, <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.11">Verr. 1.11</ref>, <ref n="pl32.3">p. 32, l. 3</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_quod_p106_l5"> , <gloss>what of this, — that</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subsellia_p106_l6">: undoubtedly wooden benches brought in for the occasion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consulares_p106_l7">: these voted as a class, and probably sat together. Catiline, as a <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetorius</foreign>, no doubt sat in their neighborhood. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ferendum_p106_l10"> [<foreign xml:lang="lat">esse</foreign>] is the pred. of the clause </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_reliquerunt_p106_l10"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servi_p106_l10">: emphatic, and hence preceding </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_p106_l10"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="injuria_p106_l13"> , <gloss>unjustly, wrongfully</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="carere_aspectu_p106_l14"> , <gloss>be deprived of the sight of</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquo_concederes_p106_l20"> , <gloss>would retire somewhere</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_p106_l20">: opposed to the cont. to fact </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_p106_l20">, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="te_cogitare_p106_l22"> , <gloss>that you think of nothing</gloss> (depending on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicat_p106_l22"> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicat_p106_l22_2">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref> (276, a); B. 259, 4; G. 230; H. 532, 2 (467, 2); H.-B. 485. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritatem_p106_l23">, etc.: observe the climax in both nouns and verbs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_p106_l25"> (i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">patria</foreign>)... <foreign xml:lang="lat">agit</foreign>, <gloss>she thus pleads with you</gloss> . <pb n="371"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="annis_p106_l26">: § <ref n="AG 424">424</ref>, b (256,b); B. 231,1; G. 393, R.2; H. 417, I and 2 (379, I); H.-B. 440. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sociorum_p106_l28">, i.e. the allied cities of the province of Africa, which Catiline had governed as <foreign xml:lang="lat">pro-praetor</foreign>, <date when="-0067">B.C. 67</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neglegandas_p106_l29"> implies only evasion; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="evertendas_p106_l29">, violence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="leges_quaestiones_p106_l29">, i.e. in his lawless career both as <foreign xml:lang="lat"> praetor</foreign> in Rome and as <foreign xml:lang="lat"> pro-praetor</foreign> in Africa. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superiora_illa_p106_l30"> , <gloss>those former crimes of yours</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_esse_p106_l32">, etc.: this and the two following infin. clauses (Catilinam </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="timeri_p106_l32"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullum_consilium_p106_l32">) are subjects of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_ferendum_p106_l32"> ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_p106_l32"> depends on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videri_p106_l32"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quicquid_increpuerit_p106_l32">, subj. of integral part; § <ref n="AG 593">593</ref> (342); B. 324, I; G. 663, I; H. 652 (529, ii); H-B. 539. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="abhorreat_p107_l2"> (subj. of characteristic), <gloss>is inconsistent with</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hunc_eripe_p107_l3"> , <gloss>rescue me from</gloss>, etc. (lit. <gloss>snatch it from me</gloss>); § <ref n="AG 381">381</ref>(229); B. 188, 2, d; G. 345, R.1; H. 429, 2 (386, 2); 14.-B. 371. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_opprimar_p107_l4">: § <ref n="AG 515">515</ref>, a (306, a); B. 302,4; G. 595; 11.580 (508, 4); H.-B. 582, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquando_p107_l4"> , <gloss>some time or other</gloss> (implying impatience). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_possit_p107_l6">: § <ref n="AG 527">527</ref>, c (313, c); cf. B. 309; G. 604 and R.1; 14.585(515, ii); H-B. 582,8. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_dedisti_p107_l7">, i.e. into free custody, on parole. This appears to have been late in October, when Catiline was prosecuted on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Lex Plautia de vi</foreign>. When a respectable Roman was charged with a crime it was customary for some person to bail him out, as it were, by becoming responsible for his appearance. Being thus responsible, the surety kept the accused in a kind of custody at his house. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_Lepidum_p107_l8">, etc.: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_p107_l8"> = <foreign xml:lang="lat">apud</foreign>. Lepidus was the consul of <date when="-0066">B.C. 66</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_me_p107_l9">: this was of course intended by Catiline as a demonstration of his innocence. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domi_meae_p107_l10">: § <ref n="AG 428">428</ref>, k (258, e); G. 411, a.4; H.-B. 454.1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parietibus_p107_l12">, loc. abl.; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="moenibus_p107_l12">, abl. of means. Observe the difference of meaning in these words and the emphasis of the contrast, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_essem_p107_l12">: this would be subj. (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sim</foreign>) in dir. disc. as <pb n="372"/> implying the reason; § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, e (320, e); B. 283, 3; G. 626, a.; H. 592, 598 (517); H.-B. 523. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Metellum_p107_l13">: Q. Metellus Celer, consul <date when="-0060">B.C. 60</date>; he afterwards did good service in the campaign against Catiline. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="virum_optimum_p107_l14"> , <gloss>an excellent man</gloss> (ironical, of course). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sagacissimum_p107_l16"> , <gloss> keen-scented</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fortissimum_p107_l16"> , <gloss>energetic and fearless</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videtur_debere_p107_l18"> , <gloss>does it seem that he ought to be?</gloss> Observe that the Latin prefers the personal construction ("does he seem," etc.), which the English idiom with <gloss>ought</gloss> does not allow us to imitate: § <ref n="AG 582">582</ref> (330, b, I); B. 332, b; G. 528, R2; H. 611, N.1 (534, 1, N.1); Cf. H-B. 590, I, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="refer_senatum_p107_l23">: the technical term for the action of the presiding officer (regularly the consul) in bringing a matter before the Senate for action. See general Introduction, p. lvii. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_placere_p107_l23">, etc.: fut. cond. in indir. disc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="placere_p107_l24"> (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">sibi</foreign>): the subject is <foreign xml:lang="lat">te</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat"> exsilium</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="abhorret_p107_l25"> , <gloss>is contrary to</gloss>: because the Senate would have no legal power to pronounce such a judgment. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faciam_ut_p107_l26">, etc.: § <ref n="AG 568">568</ref> (332); B. 297, I G. 553, I ; H. 568 (498, ii); H.-B. 502, 3, a. To make the feelings of the Senate clear, Cicero formally commands Catiline to leave the city (<foreign xml:lang="lat">egredere</foreign>, etc.); then pauses to allow the Senators a chance to protest, and then points out that no objections are heard. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ecquid_attendis_p107_l29"> , <gloss>are you listening?</gloss> The adverbial ecquid (<gloss>at all</gloss>) can hardly be idiomatically rendered, but gives an emphasis to the question. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patiuntur_p107_l30"> , <gloss>they tolerate this</gloss>, i.e. they make no objection to this extreme exercise of authority on my part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_exspectas_p107_l30">, etc.: <gloss>why do you wait for those to express their opinion in words whose wishes you see clearly by their silence?</gloss> The Latin idiom is quite different: <gloss>why do you wait for the expressed opinion</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">auctoritatem</foreign> ) <gloss>of</gloss> [those] <gloss>speaking whose wishes you see</gloss> [when] <gloss> silent?</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_p107_l32"> , <gloss>this.. . here</gloss>: the demonstrative pronouns are often thus employed in the so-called <gloss>deictic</gloss> use, accompanied by a gesture. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Sestio_p107_l32">: a member of the aristocratic party whom Cicero afterwards defended in one of his greatest orations. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Marcello_p108_l1">: a prominent member of the aristocracy, consul <date when="-0051">B.C. 51</date>; not to be confounded with the person of the same name mentioned in sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.19">19</ref>. He took a leading part in the Civil War against Caesar, and was afterwards defended by Cicero (see p.213)-jam, <gloss>by this time</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consuli_p108_l1"> , <gloss>consul as I ant</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_templo_p108_l2">, i.e. notwithstanding the sacredness of the place. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vim_manus_p108_l2"> (hendiadys), <gloss>violent hands</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_quiescunt_p108_l3">, i.e. by keeping quiet: § <ref n="AG 549">549</ref>, a (326, a); G. 582; H. 599(517,2); H-B. 551. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videlicet_cara_p108_l5">, alluding to his demand to have the matter submitted to the Senate. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voces_p108_l8"> , <gloss> cries</gloss> (of the crowd outside). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_p108_l10"> (with a gesture, cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">huic</foreign>, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.21">21</ref>, first note), i.e. all that is round us, the city, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prosequantur_p108_l12"> , <gloss> escort</gloss> It was the custom for those who were going into voluntary exile to be thus accompanied to the gate by their friends. Cicero sarcastically declares that, if Catiline will depart, the whole Senate will be so glad to be rid of him as to forget his crimes and pay him this honor. <milestone n="9" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="te_frangat_p108_l13">, [the idea] <gloss>that anything should bend you!</gloss> i.e. break down your stubbornness; an exclam. clause with <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 462">462</ref>, a (332, c); G. 558; H. 559,5(486, ii, N.); H.-B. 503 and b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ire_p108_l16">: § <ref n="AG 457">457</ref>(271, a); B. 295,4, N.; G. 532, and R.2; H. 565,5 (498, i, N.); H.-B. 586, e. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recenti_memoria_p108_l18"> (abl. of time): translate by a <gloss>while</gloss> clause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_tanti_p108_l19"> , <gloss>it is worth the cost</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 417">417</ref> (252, a); Cf. B. 203, 3; G. 380, I, R.; H. 448, 4 (405); H.-B. 356, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sit_p108_l20">: § <ref n="AG 528">528</ref>(314); B 310, ii; G. 573; H. 587 (513,i); H.-B. 529. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_commoveare_p108_l21">, etc., subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">est postulandum</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 566">566</ref> (331, h); cf. B. 295; G. 546, 1 ; H. 564, ii (499, 3); cf. H.-B. 502, 3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="is_ut_p108_l23">: § <ref n="AG 537">537</ref>, 2, N.2 (319, R.); B. 284, 1; G. 552; H. 570 (500, ii); H.-B. 521, 2, a and footnote. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inimico_p108_l26">, a <gloss>private enemy</gloss>, thus attributing to Cicero personal motives of opposition. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recta_p108_l27"> (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">via</foreign>), <gloss>straightway</gloss> . <foreign xml:lang="lat">vix feram</foreign>, etc.: for Catiline's going into voluntary exile would tend to prove that he was innocent and had been persecuted by the consul (see note on <ref n="pl107.19">p. 107, l. 19</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sin_autem_p108_l29">, etc.: Catiline's going to Manlius would prove his guilt and show the wisdom of Cicero's action. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latrocinio_p109_l1"> , <gloss> brigandage</gloss>, i.e. partisan warfare, as opposed to a regular war (<foreign xml:lang="lat">justum bellum</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_p109_l3"> , <gloss>and yet</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 527">527</ref>, d, N. (313, f); B. 309, 5; G. 605, R.2; H. 586, 4 (515, iii, N.2); H.-B. 310, 7. cf. the same use of <foreign xml:lang="lat"> quamquam</foreign>, <ref n="pl108.13">p. 108, l. 13</ref>, and of <foreign xml:lang="lat"> tametsi</foreign>, <ref n="pl108.16">p. 108, l. 16</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invitem_p109_l3">: § <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 265; H. 557(486, ii); H.-B. 503. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sciam_p109_l3">: characteristic subj. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Forum_Aurelium_p109_l4">: a small place on the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Via Aurelia</foreign>, about fifty miles from Rome. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">Via Aurelia</foreign> was the road which led along the sea-coast of Etruria, by which Catiline left the city the following night. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praestolarentur_p109_l4">: rel. clause of purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aquilam_p109_l6">: the silver eagle had been adopted by Marius as the standard of the legion, and the eagle in question was said to have been actually used in the army of Marius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sacrarium_p109_l8">: it was customary in Roman houses to have a little shrine (see Fig. 27) for the worship of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lares</foreign> and other protecting divinities. Doubtless Catiline was believed to have placed this eagle in such a shrine as an object of superstitious worship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possis_p109_l9">: exclam. clause with <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut</foreign> (see note on <ref n="pl108.13">p. 108, l. 13</ref>). <milestone n="10" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rapiebat_p109_l13">: § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, b (277, b); B. 260, 4; H-B. 485; the imperf. is used instead of the pres. because the action is conceived of as ceasing at the moment when Cicero discovered the plot. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_res_p109_l14">, i.e. leaving the city as an enemy and taking up arms. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_modo_p109_l16"> , <gloss>to say nothing of</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 327">327</ref>, I (209, a, I); B. 347, 2; G. 445; H. 656, 2 (553, 2); H.-B. 298, 2, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_p109_l18"> connects </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perditis_p109_l18"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="derelictis_p109_l18"> ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_p109_l18"> connects </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fortuna_p109_l18"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="spe_p109_l18"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="derelictis_p109_l18_2"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conflatam_p109_l19"> , <gloss>run together</gloss> (like molten metal). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_p109_l19">, i.e. in this band. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bacchabere_p109_l21"> , <gloss>will revel</gloss>. To a Roman the word suggested the wild orgies of the frenzied Bacchanals, so that it is much stronger than our <gloss> revel</gloss>, which in course of time has become rather vague: cf. Aeneid 4.301 (and illustrations). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meditati_sunt_p109_l23"> , <gloss>have been practised</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="feruntur_p109_l23"> , <gloss>are talked about</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="labores_p109_l23">: cf. <ref>Sallust Catiline 5</ref>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">L. Catilina nobili genere natus fuit, magna vi et animi et corporis, sed ingenio malo pravoque. Huic ab adulescentia bella intestina caedes rapinae discordia civilis grata fuere ibique juventutem suam exercuit. Corpus patiens inediae algoris vigiliae supra quam cuiquam credibile est</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facinus_p109_l25"> , <gloss>deed of violence</gloss>, contrasted with <foreign xml:lang="lat"> stuprum</foreign> , <gloss>debauchery</gloss>; just as <foreign xml:lang="lat">bonis otiosorum</foreign>, <gloss>property of peaceful citizens</gloss>, is with <foreign xml:lang="lat">somno maritorum</foreign>, <gloss>the repose of husbands</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ubi_ostentes_p109_l26"> (purpose clause), <gloss>opportunity to display</gloss> (lit. a place, <gloss> where</gloss>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reppuli_p109_l29">: § <ref n="AG 545">545</ref>(323, I) B. 288, I, a; G. 580; H. 600 (5 1, i); H.-B. 550 and a. Cicero here takes credit to himself for using his influence as consul to defeat the election of Catiline. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exsul_p109_l30"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consul_p109_l30">: observe the play upon words (see Vocab.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latrocinium_p109_l32">: cf. note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">latrocinio</foreign>, l. 1, above. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="querimoniam_p110_l2">, i.e. for not having suppressed the conspiracy more vigorously. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="detester_deprecer_p110_l2"> (construed with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_me_p110_l2">, above), <gloss>remove by protest and plea</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patria_p110_l4">: the personified <foreign xml:lang="lat">patria</foreign> is dramatically introduced as accusing Cicero of remissness in letting Catiline go unharmed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Tulli_p110_l6"> (voc.): the regular way of formal address; the use of the family name (<foreign xml:lang="lat">Cicero</foreign>) is more familiar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="evocatorem_servorum_p110_l9"> , <gloss>a summoner of slaves</gloss>, i.e. to enlist under him against the state. To the Romans (as to all peoples who, having a large slave population, are in constant fear of servile revolts) such an accusation was the most violent reproach conceivable. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duci_mactari_p110_l12">; § <ref n="AG 563">563</ref>, a, N. (311, a, N.1); Cf. B. 295, 4, N.; G. 546, N.3; H. 614 (535, ii); H-B. 587, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rogatae_sunt_p110_l16">: the magistrate who proposed a law formally asked the people whether they would accept it; hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">rogo</foreign> was the word regularly used for this act, and the proposition itself was called <foreign xml:lang="lat">rogatio</foreign>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">leges</foreign> in question, <foreign xml:lang="lat">Valeria, Porcia</foreign>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sempronia</foreign> (of Caius Gracchus), were enacted to protect — like our laws securing the <foreign xml:lang="lat">habeas corpus</foreign> and trial by jury — the life and liberty of citizens against the arbitrary power of magistrates, which in this case would apparently be used by Cicero. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_numquam_p110_l16">, etc.: as a fact, however, the precedents here referred to had been really violations of the constitution. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeclaram_gratiam_p110_l18"> , <gloss>you show a noble gratitude</gloss> (cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">habere gratiam</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">agere gratias</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nulla_majorum_p110_l20">: though by the Roman constitution the higher offices were open to all citizens, yet it was rare that a man whose ancestors had not held these offices could succeed in attaining them himself. If, like Cicero, he did so, he was called a <foreign xml:lang="lat">novus homo</foreign>, and his descendants belonged to the nobility. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_mature_p110_l20">: Cicero attained the quaestorship, the praetorship, and the consulship (<foreign xml:lang="lat">honorum gradiis</foreign>) at the earliest age possible in each case. This was a mark of public confidence which had never happened to a <foreign xml:lang="lat">novus homo</foreign> before. <pb n="377"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invidiae_p110_l22">, i.e. the odium which might attach to the consuls apparently exceeding his constitutional authority. In fact Cicero was later brought to trial and exiled on this very charge. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="num_est_p110_l24"> , <gloss>pray is</gloss> (implying strong negation): § <ref n="AG 332"> 332</ref>, b (210, c); B. 162,2, b; G. 464, R.; H. 378(351, I, N.8); H-B. 231, I, (1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inertiae_p110_l25">, (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">invidia</foreign>), the <gloss> reproach</gloss> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="an_p110_l25"> belongs with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_existimas_p110_l25"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conflagraturum_p110_l27"> , <gloss>will be consumed</gloss> (lit. <gloss>will burn up</gloss> ). <milestone n="110.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_sentiunt_p110_l27"> , <gloss>have the same views</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mentibus_p110_l27"> , <gloss> thoughts</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="factu_p110_l30">, the rare "latter supine": § <ref n="AG 510">510</ref> (303); B. 340, 2; G. 436; H. 635 and 4 (547 and N.1); H-B. 619, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gladiatori_p110_l31">: the gladiators were trained slaves owned by rich men, and were often employed as bullies in political campaigns. Hence the word came almost to mean <gloss>ruffian, "bruiser," "thug."</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_honestarunt_p111_l1">: notice that the simple condition here expresses cause; § <ref n="AG 515">515</ref>, a, N. (306, a, N.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superiorum_p111_l2"> , <gloss>before them</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_pervenerit_p111_l2"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fore_p111_l2"> (fut., indir. disc.), ll. 15, 16; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_interfecto_p111_l2"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_p111_l2"> (fut., indir. disc., <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> disguised), ll. 18-20; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_exstinguetur_p111_l2"> (fut., more vivid), ll.20-22. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maxime_p111_l6"> , <gloss>ever so much</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_putarem_p111_l7">, result clause explaining hoc (not a subst. clause). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="partam_p111_l7"> (from <foreign xml:lang="lat">pario</foreign>), <gloss>acquired</gloss> (a very common meaning). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_nulli_p111_l8">, etc.: it should be remembered that there were many well-intentioned citizens who either doubted the existence of a conspiracy or thought Cicero's fears of it greatly exaggerated; and that' even among those who admitted the fact there was considerable variety of partisan feeling. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videant_p111_l9"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">dissimulent</foreign>: subj. of charact. (not coord. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicerent_p111_l9">), expressing the <emph>character</emph> of the men referred to, while </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aluerunt_p111_l9">, etc. (l. 11), merely gives additional facts about them (hence indic.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="regie_p111_l14"> , <gloss> despotically</gloss>: the Roman idea of a king and kingly government was associated with Tarquinius Superbus. Here the word also implies the assumption of unlawful power (= <foreign xml:lang="lat">tyrannice</foreign>), as well as its abuse. <pb n="378"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_p111_l15"> , <gloss>as it is</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_p111_l15">: § <ref n="AG 308">308</ref>, g (201,f 2); cf. G. 611, R.1; the antecedent is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_castra_p111_l15"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="improbum_p111_l17"> , <gloss> dishonest</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_interfecto_p111_l18">, disguised fut. <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, a (310, a); B. 305, I; G. 600, I; H. 575, 9 (507, N.7); H-B. 578, 6; the apod. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_p111_l18"> is fut. in sense: § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, d, 584, b (307, d); G. 248, R.; H.-B. 472, d. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejecerit_p111_l20">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, c (307, c); G. 244, 2; H. 574, 2 (508, 2); Cf. H.-B. 577, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eodem_p111_l21"> , <gloss>to the same place</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adulta_p111_l22"> , <gloss> full-grown</gloss>, as opposed to <foreign xml:lang="lat"> stirps</foreign> , <gloss>the root</gloss> (properly the <gloss> stock</gloss> from which new shoots may spring out), and semen, the seed. <milestone n="13" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_diu_p111_l25">: the conspiracy was ready to break out <date when="-0065">B.C. 65</date> (see note on <ref n="pl105.15">p. 105, l. 15</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versamur_p111_l26"> , <gloss>have lived</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nescio_pacto_p111_l26"> , <gloss>somehow or other</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 575">575</ref>, d (344, a); B. 253, 6; G. 467, N.; Cf. H. 512, 7 (455, 2); H-B. 537, e. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veteris_p111_l27"> (sharply contrasted with <foreign xml:lang="lat">nostri</foreign>), i.e. the disease is of long standing, but its outbreak has occurred just in my consulship. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="visceribus_p111_l32"> , <gloss> vitals</gloss> (properly the great interior organs, as the heart, lungs, etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aestu_febrique_p112_l1"> , <gloss>the heat of fever</gloss> (hendiadys). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reliquis_vivis_p112_l4">: abl. absolute. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="circumstare_p112_l8"> , <gloss>hang round</gloss>, for the purpose of intimidation: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor urbanus</foreign> had his tribunal in the Forum. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patefacta_p112_l15"> , <gloss>laid bare</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inlustrata_p112_l15"> , <gloss>set in full light</gloss>; <foreign xml:lang="lat"> oppressa</foreign> , <gloss>crushed</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vindicata_p112_l15"> , <gloss> punished</gloss>. Observe the climax. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ominibus_p113_l1"> , <gloss> prospects</gloss>. What Cicero has just said (p.112,11. I 1-16) makes the <foreign xml:lang="lat">omen</foreign> under which Catiline is to depart, — an omen of good for the state, but of evil for him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Juppiter_p113_l4">: thus the oration closes with a prayer to Jupiter Stator, in whose temple the Senate was now assembled. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Statorem_p113_l6"> , <gloss>the Stay</gloss>. The name was apparently first given to Jupiter as the <gloss> Stayer</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> sto</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sisto</foreign>) of flight (see note to sect. 11, p.104, 1. I), but it is here applied to him as the <gloss> Stay</gloss> (supporter) of the Roman state, a meaning which the word may well have from its derivation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="arcebis_p113_l8">, used as a mild imperative; § <ref n="AG 449">449</ref>, b (269,f); B. 261, 3; G. 2651; H. 560, 4, N. (487, 4); H.-B. 572. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latrones_p113_l9">: cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">latrocinium</foreign> in sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.27">27</ref> (<ref n="pl109.32">p. 109, l. 32</ref> ). </gloss>
